The **House of Representatives** (衆議院, *Shūgiin*; Japanese pronunciation: [[ɕɯː.ɡʲiꜜ.iɴ, -ŋʲiꜜ-]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")[[1]](#cite_note-3)) is the [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house") of the [National Diet](/wiki/National_Diet "National Diet") of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"). The [House of Councillors](/wiki/House_of_Councillors "House of Councillors") is the [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house"). The composition of the House is established by [Article 41](/w/index.php?title=Article_41_of_the_Constitution_of_Japan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Article 41 of the Constitution of Japan (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%97%A5%E6%9C%AC%E5%9B%BD%E6%86%B2%E6%B3%95%E7%AC%AC41%E6%9D%A1 "ja:日本国憲法第41条")] and [Article 42](/w/index.php?title=Article_42_of_the_Constitution_of_Japan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Article 42 of the Constitution of Japan (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%97%A5%E6%9C%AC%E5%9B%BD%E6%86%B2%E6%B3%95%E7%AC%AC42%E6%9D%A1 "ja:日本国憲法第42条")] of the [Constitution of Japan](/wiki/Constitution_of_Japan "Constitution of Japan"). The House of Representatives has 465 members, elected for a four-year term. Of these, 176 members are elected from 11 multi-member constituencies by a [party-list system](/wiki/Party-list_proportional_representation "Party-list proportional representation") of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"), and 289 are elected from single-member constituencies.

The overall voting system used to elect the House of Representatives is a [parallel system](/wiki/Parallel_voting "Parallel voting"), a form of [semi-proportional representation](/wiki/Semi-proportional_representation "Semi-proportional representation"). Under a parallel system, the allocation of list seats does not take into account the outcome in the single seat constituencies. Therefore, the overall allocation of seats in the House of Representatives is not fully proportional, to the advantage of larger parties.

The House of Representatives is the more powerful of the two houses, able to override vetoes on bills imposed by the House of Councillors with a two-thirds majority. In the case of [treaties](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty"), the [budget](/wiki/Government_budget "Government budget"), and the selection of the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Japan "Prime Minister of Japan"), the House of Councillors can only delay passage, but not block the legislation. The [most recent election](/wiki/2026_Japanese_general_election "2026 Japanese general election") for the House of Representatives was held on February 8, 2026, in which the [Liberal Democratic Party](/wiki/Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)") won a super-majority of seats, over 316; in the [previous election](/wiki/2024_Japanese_general_election "2024 Japanese general election"), they had failed to reach a majority of 233 seats, instead winning 191.

## Right to vote and candidature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Representatives_(Japan)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Right to vote and candidature")]

The composition of the House is established by [Article 41](/w/index.php?title=Article_41_of_the_Constitution_of_Japan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Article 41 of the Constitution of Japan (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%97%A5%E6%9C%AC%E5%9B%BD%E6%86%B2%E6%B3%95%E7%AC%AC41%E6%9D%A1 "ja:日本国憲法第41条")] and [Article 42](/w/index.php?title=Article_42_of_the_Constitution_of_Japan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Article 42 of the Constitution of Japan (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%97%A5%E6%9C%AC%E5%9B%BD%E6%86%B2%E6%B3%95%E7%AC%AC42%E6%9D%A1 "ja:日本国憲法第42条")] of the [Constitution of Japan](/wiki/Constitution_of_Japan "Constitution of Japan").[[2]](#cite_note-4)

* Japanese nationals aged 18 years and older may vote (prior to 2016, the voting age was 20).[[3]](#cite_note-5)
* Japanese nationals aged 25 years and older may run for office in the lower house.

## Differences between the Upper and Lower Houses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Representatives_(Japan)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Differences between the Upper and Lower Houses")]

The House of Representatives is the more powerful of the two houses. The House of Representatives has several powers not given to the House of Councillors. If a bill is passed by the [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house") (the House of Representatives) but is voted down by the [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house") (the [House of Councillors](/wiki/House_of_Councillors "House of Councillors")) the House of Representatives can override the decision of the House of Councillors by a two-thirds vote in the affirmative. However, in the case of [treaties](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty"), the [budget](/wiki/Government_budget "Government budget"), and the selection of the prime minister, the House of Councillors can only delay passage, but not block the legislation. As a result, the House of Representatives is considered the more powerful house.[[4]](#cite_note-6)[[5]](#cite_note-7)[[6]](#cite_note-8)

Members of the House of Representatives, who are elected to a maximum of four years, sit for a shorter term than members of the House of Councillors, who are elected to full six-year terms. The lower house can also be dissolved by the Prime Minister or the passage of a nonconfidence motion, while the House of Councillors cannot be dissolved. Thus the House of Representatives is considered to be more sensitive to public opinion, and is termed the "lower house". While the legislative term is nominally 4 years, early elections for the lower house are very common, and the median lifespan of postwar legislatures has in practice been around 3 years.

Shaded

* green: Ruling party/coalition before and after the lower house election
* red: Ruling party/coalition *until* the election = Change of government as a result of the lower house election
* blue: Ruling party/coalition *after* the election = Change of government as a result of the lower house election

*Note that the composition of the ruling coalition may change between lower house elections, e.g. after upper house elections. Parties who vote with the government in the Diet, but are not part of the cabinet (e.g. SDP & NPH after the 1996 election) are not shaded.*

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Representatives_(Japan)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

### Meiji period (1890–1912)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Representatives_(Japan)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Meiji period (1890–1912)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Kiyotaka_Kuroda_2.jpg/250px-Kiyotaka_Kuroda_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kiyotaka_Kuroda_2.jpg)

[Kuroda Kiyotaka](/wiki/Kuroda_Kiyotaka "Kuroda Kiyotaka"), [Satsuma](/wiki/Satsuma_Domain "Satsuma Domain") [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") and prime minister in the late 1880s, coined the term "transcendentalism" (超然主義, chōzen shugi) on the occasion of the promulgation of the Meiji Constitution in 1889. The oligarchs should try to "transcend" electoral politics and govern without partisan majorities the House of Representatives.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/IT%C5%8C_Hirobumi.jpg/250px-IT%C5%8C_Hirobumi.jpg)](/wiki/File:IT%C5%8C_Hirobumi.jpg)

[Itō Hirobumi](/wiki/It%C5%8D_Hirobumi "Itō Hirobumi"), a [Chōshū](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_Domain "Chōshū Domain") samurai, member of the [House of Peers](/wiki/House_of_Peers_(Japan) "House of Peers (Japan)") and [prime minister of Japan](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Japan "Prime Minister of Japan") on three non-consecutive occasions between 1885 and 1901. He was a main architect of the Imperial Constitution which created the Imperial Diet. When the oligarchs attempts to govern "transcendentally" mostly failed in the 1890s, he saw the necessity for permanent allies among elected political parties.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Takashi_Hara_posing.jpg/250px-Takashi_Hara_posing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Takashi_Hara_posing.jpg)

[Hara Takashi](/wiki/Hara_Takashi "Hara Takashi"), although born a [Morioka](/wiki/Morioka_Domain "Morioka Domain") noble, made his career as commoner-politician and became the first and one of only three prime ministers from the House of Representatives in the Empire.

See also: [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration")

The Japanese parliament, then known as the Imperial Diet, was established in 1890 as a result of the 1889 [Meiji Constitution](/wiki/Meiji_Constitution "Meiji Constitution"). It was modeled on the parliaments of several Western countries, particularly the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") and the United Kingdom, because of the [Emperor Meiji](/wiki/Emperor_Meiji "Emperor Meiji")'s westernizing reforms. The Imperial Diet consisted of [two chambers](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism"), the elected House of Representatives which was the lower house, and the [House of Peers](/wiki/House_of_Peers_(Japan) "House of Peers (Japan)") which was the upper house. This format was similar to the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") in the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), or the [Herrenhaus](/wiki/Prussian_House_of_Lords "Prussian House of Lords") in [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia"), where the upper house represented the [aristocracy](/wiki/Aristocracy_(government) "Aristocracy (government)").

Both houses, and also the Emperor, had to agree on legislation, and even at the height of party-based constitutional government, the House of Peers could simply vote down bills deemed too liberal by the [Meiji oligarchy](/wiki/Meiji_oligarchy "Meiji oligarchy"), such as the introduction of [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage"), increases in local autonomy, or trade union rights. The [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Japan "Prime Minister of Japan") and his government served [at the Emperor's pleasure](/wiki/At_Her_Majesty%27s_pleasure "At Her Majesty's pleasure"), and could not be removed by the Imperial Diet. However, the right to vote on, and if necessary to block, legislation including the budget, gave the House of Representatives leverage to force the government into negotiations. After an early period of frequent confrontation and temporary alliances between the cabinet and political parties in the lower house, parts of the Meiji oligarchy more sympathetic to political parties around [Itō Hirobumi](/wiki/It%C5%8D_Hirobumi "Itō Hirobumi") and parts of the liberal parties eventually formed a more permanent alliance, in the form of the [Rikken Seiyūkai](/wiki/Rikken_Seiy%C5%ABkai "Rikken Seiyūkai") in 1900. The confidence of the House of Representatives was never a formal requirement to govern, but between 1905 and 1918, only one cabinet took office that did not enjoy majority support in the House of Representatives.[[16]](#cite_note-19)

### Taisho and early Showa periods (1912–1937)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Representatives_(Japan)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Taisho and early Showa periods (1912–1937)")]

See also: [Politics of the Empire of Japan (1914–1944)](/wiki/Politics_of_the_Empire_of_Japan_(1914%E2%80%931944) "Politics of the Empire of Japan (1914–1944)")

During the [Taishō political crisis](/wiki/Taish%C5%8D_political_crisis "Taishō political crisis") in 1913, a [no-confidence vote](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence")[[17]](#cite_note-20) against the [third Katsura government](/wiki/Katsura_Tar%C5%8D#Third_administration "Katsura Tarō"), accompanied by major demonstrations outside the Diet, was followed shortly by resignation. Subsequently, in the period often referred to as [Taishō democracy](/wiki/Taish%C5%8D_democracy "Taishō democracy"), it became increasingly customary to appoint many ministers, including several prime ministers, from the House of Representatives – [Hara Takashi](/wiki/Hara_Takashi "Hara Takashi") was the first commoner to become prime minister in 1918.

In the same year, the [Rice Riots](/wiki/Rice_Riots_of_1918 "Rice Riots of 1918") had confronted the government with an unprecedented scale of domestic unrest, and a [German Revolution](/wiki/German_Revolution_of_1918%E2%80%931919 "German Revolution of 1918–1919") brought the Prusso-German monarchy to an end, the very system Meiji oligarchs had used as the main model for the Meiji constitution to consolidate and preserve Imperial power. Even [Yamagata Aritomo](/wiki/Yamagata_Aritomo "Yamagata Aritomo") and other oligarchs that had been fundamentally opposed to political parties, became more inclined to cooperate with the still mainly [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") parties, to prevent a rise of socialism or other movements that might threaten Imperial rule. Socialist parties would not be represented in significant numbers in the lower house until the 1930s.

The initially very high [census suffrage](/wiki/Suffrage#Census_suffrage "Suffrage") requirement was reduced several times, until the introduction of [universal male suffrage](/wiki/Universal_manhood_suffrage "Universal manhood suffrage") in 1925. The electoral system to the House of Representatives was also fundamentally changed several times: between systems of "small" mostly single- and few multi-member electoral districts (1890s, 1920, 1924), "medium" mostly multi-member districts (1928–1942) and "large" electoral districts (usually only one, rarely two city and one counties district per prefecture; 1900s and 1910s), using [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") in single-member districts, [plurality-at-large voting](/wiki/Plurality-at-large_voting "Plurality-at-large voting") (1890s) or [single non-transferable vote](/wiki/Single_non-transferable_vote "Single non-transferable vote") in the multi-member districts.

Influence of the House of Representatives on the government increased, and the party cabinets of the 1920s brought Japan apparently closer to a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") of government, and there were several reforms to the upper house in 1925. However, the balance of powers between the two houses and the influential role of extra-constitutional actors such as the [Genrō](/wiki/Genr%C5%8D "Genrō") (who still selected the prime minister) or the military (that had brought down several cabinets) remained in essence untouched. Within a year of the [Japanese invasion of Manchuria](/wiki/Japanese_invasion_of_Manchuria "Japanese invasion of Manchuria") in September 1931, a series of assassinations and coup attempts followed. Party governments were replaced by [governments of "national unity"](/wiki/National_unity_government "National unity government") (*kyokoku itchi*) which were dominated by nobles, bureaucrats and increasingly the military.

### World War II and aftermath (1937–1947)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Representatives_(Japan)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: World War II and aftermath (1937–1947)")]

See also: [Politics of the Empire of Japan (1914–1944)](/wiki/Politics_of_the_Empire_of_Japan_(1914%E2%80%931944) "Politics of the Empire of Japan (1914–1944)")

After the [Marco Polo Bridge Incident](/wiki/Marco_Polo_Bridge_Incident "Marco Polo Bridge Incident") and the start of war in 1937, the influence of the Imperial Diet was further diminished, though never eliminated, by special laws such as the [National Mobilization Law](/wiki/National_Mobilization_Law "National Mobilization Law") and expanded powers for cabinet agencies such as the Planning Board.[[18]](#cite_note-21) The House of Representatives in the Empire had a four-year term and could be dissolved by the [Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan"). In contrast, members of the House of Peers had either [life tenure](/wiki/Life_tenure "Life tenure") (subject to revocation by the Emperor) or a seven-year term in the case of members elected in mutual peerage elections among the three lower peerage ranks, top taxpayer and academic peerage elections. During the war, the term of the members of the House of Representatives elected in the last pre-war [election of 1937](/wiki/1937_Japanese_general_election "1937 Japanese general election") was extended by one year.

In the [1946 election to the House of Representatives](/wiki/1946_Japanese_general_election "1946 Japanese general election"), held under the U.S.-led [Allied occupation of Japan](/wiki/Occupation_of_Japan "Occupation of Japan"), [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage") was introduced, and a system of "large" electoral districts (one or two per prefecture) with [limited voting](/wiki/Limited_voting "Limited voting") was used. A change in the electoral law in April 1945 had for the first time allocated 30 seats to the established colonies of the Empire: Karafuto ([Sakhalin](/wiki/Sakhalin "Sakhalin")), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan_under_Japanese_rule "Taiwan under Japanese rule"), and Chōsen ([Korea](/wiki/Korea_under_Japanese_rule "Korea under Japanese rule")); but this change was never implemented. Similarly, Korea and Taiwan were granted several appointed members of the [House of Peers](/wiki/House_of_Peers_(Japan) "House of Peers (Japan)") in 1945.

In 1946, both houses of the Imperial Diet (together with the Emperor) passed the [postwar constitutional amendment](/wiki/Constitution_of_Japan "Constitution of Japan") which took effect in 1947. The Imperial Diet was renamed the National Diet, the House of Peers was replaced by an elected upper house called the [House of Councillors](/wiki/House_of_Councillors "House of Councillors"), and the House of Representatives would now be able to override the upper house in important matters. The constitution also gave the Diet exclusive legislative authority, without involvement of the Emperor, and explicitly made the cabinet responsible to the Diet and requires that the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Japan "Prime Minister of Japan") has the support of a majority in the House of Representatives.

### Late Showa period (1947–1989)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Representatives_(Japan)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Late Showa period (1947–1989)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Shigeru_Yoshida_suit.jpg/250px-Shigeru_Yoshida_suit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shigeru_Yoshida_suit.jpg)

[Shigeru Yoshida](/wiki/Shigeru_Yoshida "Shigeru Yoshida"), prime minister 1946–1947 as a member of the House of Peers and 1948–1954 as a member of the House of Representatives, oversaw the end of the [American-led occupation](/wiki/Occupation_of_Japan "Occupation of Japan") and the beginning of the [Japanese economic miracle](/wiki/Japanese_economic_miracle "Japanese economic miracle").

See also: [Post-occupation Japan](/wiki/Post-occupation_Japan "Post-occupation Japan")

The Diet first met under the new constitution on May 20, 1947.[[19]](#cite_note-22) Four days later, [Tetsu Katayama](/wiki/Tetsu_Katayama "Tetsu Katayama") of the [Democratic Socialist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Socialist_Party_(Japan) "Democratic Socialist Party (Japan)") became Japan's first socialist prime minister and the first since the introduction of parliamentarianism.

Since the end of US rule in 1952, it has been the norm that the prime minister dissolves the House of Representatives before its 4-year term expires. Only once, in 1976, did the House last a full 4 years. It has become tradition to give nicknames to each dissolution, usually referencing a major political issue or controversy. One infamous example was on March 14, 1953, when [Shigeru Yoshida](/wiki/Shigeru_Yoshida "Shigeru Yoshida") dissolved the House and called for new election, after he name called people during a meeting of the budget committee. This came to be known as the *["you idiot" dissolution](/w/index.php?title=%22you_idiot%22_dissolution&action=edit&redlink=1 "\"you idiot\" dissolution (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%90%E3%82%AB%E3%83%A4%E3%83%AD%E3%83%BC%E8%A7%A3%E6%95%A3 "ja:バカヤロー解散")]*.[[20]](#cite_note-23)

In 1955, prime minister [Ichirō Hatoyama](/wiki/Ichir%C5%8D_Hatoyama "Ichirō Hatoyama") oversaw the creation of the [Liberal Democratic Party](/wiki/Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)") (LDP), which since [his third government](/wiki/Third_Ichir%C5%8D_Hatoyama_Cabinet "Third Ichirō Hatoyama Cabinet") has dominated Japanese politics under the [1955 System](/wiki/1955_System "1955 System"). The LDP would govern without interruption for nearly 40 years until the [1993 election](/wiki/1993_Japanese_general_election "1993 Japanese general election"), alone save for a three-year coalition government with the [New Liberal Club](/wiki/New_Liberal_Club "New Liberal Club") after the [1983 election](/wiki/1983_Japanese_general_election "1983 Japanese general election").

Hatoyama planned to change the electoral system to [first past the post](/wiki/First_past_the_post "First past the post"), introducing a bill to that effect in March 1956. This was met with opposition from the [Socialist Party](/wiki/Japan_Socialist_Party "Japan Socialist Party"), who criticized Hatoyama's plan as a "[Hatomander](/wiki/Hatomander "Hatomander")". The bill passed the House of Representatives in May 1956, but was never voted on by the House of Councillors. Electoral reform came into vogue again in the 1970s, but [Kakuei Tanaka](/wiki/Kakuei_Tanaka "Kakuei Tanaka")'s plan met opposition internally in the LDP and never came to a vote in either chamber of the Diet.

### Heisei and Reiwa periods (since 1989)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Representatives_(Japan)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Heisei and Reiwa periods (since 1989)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Shinzo_Abe%2C_2016.jpg/250px-Shinzo_Abe%2C_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shinzo_Abe,_2016.jpg)

[Shinzo Abe](/wiki/Shinzo_Abe "Shinzo Abe"), prime minister 2006–2007 and again 2012–2020, was the longest-serving PM in Japanese history.

Japan entered a lengthy recession in the 1990s (see [Lost Decades](/wiki/Lost_Decades "Lost Decades")), which many people blamed on the LDP.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the [1993 election](/wiki/1993_Japanese_general_election "1993 Japanese general election"), the party lost power for the first time under the 1955 System, when an eight-party coalition led by [Morihiro Hosokawa](/wiki/Morihiro_Hosokawa "Morihiro Hosokawa") of the [Japan New Party](/wiki/Japan_New_Party "Japan New Party") were able to form [a government](/wiki/Hosokawa_Cabinet "Hosokawa Cabinet"). This government fell apart after nine months, and was succeeded by the [Hata Cabinet](/wiki/Hata_Cabinet "Hata Cabinet"), another short-lived non-LDP government. The LDP returned to power in 1994 with the [Murayama Cabinet](/wiki/Murayama_Cabinet "Murayama Cabinet"), this time in a coalition with their old rivals the Socialists, whose leader [Tomiichi Murayama](/wiki/Tomiichi_Murayama "Tomiichi Murayama") became prime minister.

As with party colleagues [Ichirō Hatoyama](/wiki/Ichir%C5%8D_Hatoyama "Ichirō Hatoyama") and [Kakuei Tanaka](/wiki/Kakuei_Tanaka "Kakuei Tanaka") before him, prime minister [Toshiki Kaifu](/wiki/Toshiki_Kaifu "Toshiki Kaifu") of the LDP unsuccessfully tried to reform the electoral system in 1991. However, the Morihiro Hosokawa government got the [1994 Japanese electoral reform](/wiki/1994_Japanese_electoral_reform "1994 Japanese electoral reform") through the Diet, introducing a [parallel voting](/wiki/Parallel_voting "Parallel voting") system which went into effect at the [next election in 1996](/wiki/1996_Japanese_general_election "1996 Japanese general election"). Under this system, which remains in effect as of 2022, 300 (since reduced to 289) members of the House of Representatives are elected using [first past the post](/wiki/First_past_the_post "First past the post") in single-member constituencies, while 200 (since reduced to 176) members are elected in regional blocs using [party-list proportional representation](/wiki/Party-list_proportional_representation "Party-list proportional representation").

Prime minister [Junichiro Koizumi](/wiki/Junichiro_Koizumi "Junichiro Koizumi") introduced a bill to the House of Representatives in 2006 on changing the [Imperial Household Law](/wiki/Imperial_Household_Law "Imperial Household Law") to allow a woman to ascend the [Chrysanthemum Throne](/wiki/Chrysanthemum_Throne "Chrysanthemum Throne") (see [Japanese imperial succession debate](/wiki/Japanese_imperial_succession_debate "Japanese imperial succession debate")), but he withdrew the bill after the birth of [Prince Hisahito of Akishino](/wiki/Prince_Hisahito_of_Akishino "Prince Hisahito of Akishino") the same year. The LDP once again lost power at the [2009 election](/wiki/2009_Japanese_general_election "2009 Japanese general election"), when the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_of_Japan "Democratic Party of Japan")-led [Hatoyama Cabinet](/wiki/Yukio_Hatoyama_Cabinet "Yukio Hatoyama Cabinet") took over, followed in rapid succession by the [Kan Cabinet](/wiki/Kan_Cabinet "Kan Cabinet") and [Noda Cabinet](/wiki/Noda_Cabinet "Noda Cabinet"). The LDP and [Komeito](/wiki/Komeito "Komeito"), who had formed a two-party government between 2003 and 2009, came to power again after the [2012 election](/wiki/2012_Japanese_general_election "2012 Japanese general election"). [Shinzo Abe](/wiki/Shinzo_Abe "Shinzo Abe"), who had previously led the [First Abe Cabinet](/wiki/First_Abe_Cabinet "First Abe Cabinet"), was prime minister for another stint lasting eight years, stepping down for health reasons in 2020. He was succeeded by [Yoshihide Suga](/wiki/Yoshihide_Suga "Yoshihide Suga").

When the Emperor [Akihito](/wiki/Akihito "Akihito") expressed interest in abdicating, the Diet passed the [Emperor Abdication Law](/wiki/Emperor_Abdication_Law "Emperor Abdication Law") in 2017, allowing for the [2019 Japanese imperial transition](/wiki/2019_Japanese_imperial_transition "2019 Japanese imperial transition") and the succession to the throne of [Naruhito](/wiki/Naruhito "Naruhito"). In December 2022, in light of the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine") and increased [military cooperation between China and Russia](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Russia_relations#Military_relations "China–Russia relations"), prime minister [Fumio Kishida](/wiki/Fumio_Kishida "Fumio Kishida") announced plans to significantly increase funding for the [Japan Self-Defense Forces](/wiki/Japan_Self-Defense_Forces "Japan Self-Defense Forces"); this was continued under his successor as prime minister, [Shigeru Ishiba](/wiki/Shigeru_Ishiba "Shigeru Ishiba").
